stories.title: The Bone-Thief of Greyport
stories.text:
The harbormaster's vault lay three stories beneath Greyport's Saltmere Quarter, sealed with bone-locks carved by dead smiths. Marin the Copper-Handed had stolen from every merchant house along the Iron Coast—but never from the dead. Her contract was simple: retrieve the Tidecaller's Key before the deepkin captain arrived to claim it. Pay was two consciousness-fragments and passage north, away from the Dominion auditors who'd begun asking questions about her methods. The vault's guardian was a woman made of coral and rage, her eyes still luminous with drowned memory. She'd been a harbormaster once, sworn to protect the Key with her last breath. Death hadn't released her from the oath. "I'm not here to steal from you," Marin said, showing her empty hands. "The Key sings for the tide-touched. Your captain died protecting it. Let me return it to one who'll honor that debt."

The ghost-harbormaster's form rippled, uncertain. Oaths were chains, even past death—especially then. Marin understood chains. She'd worn them her whole life, invisible as shadow. When the coral-woman stepped aside, Marin didn't run.

stories.title: The Wyvern's Reckoning
stories.text:
Sorrath had guarded the same mountain lair for three hundred years, and in that time gold had become his religion. Coins stacked like cordwood, gems the size of fists, crowns of forgotten kings—all arranged with mathematical precision, each piece catalogued in a ledger he maintained in his own talons. Gold turned to inert dust. Consciousness-fragments became currency. Sorrath's hoard became a monument to obsolescence. For weeks he lay coiled atop his worthless wealth, grieving not the loss but the betrayal. He'd done everything right—hoarded ruthlessly, protected fiercely, sacrificed comfort for accumulation. The world was supposed to reward such discipline. Instead, it had rewritten the rules while he slept. A young merchant stumbled into his lair, fleeing auditors. Her consciousness was fragmented—she'd sold pieces of herself to survive the Burned Expanse crossing. She was half-lucid, missing memories she couldn't name. "Please," she whispered, seeing only the dragon, not the mountains of gold. "I need shelter. I can pay—"

Sorrath could devour her. Consume her remaining fragments, add them to his new understanding of value. Easy prey.

stories.title: The Fae-Bride's Bargain
stories.text:
Lissaen emerged from the Twilight Court with a queen's ransom in glamour and a terrible purpose: to marry the mortal lord and bind his kingdom to the fae realm through bloodright and love-oath. Lord Vethric was handsome in the way mountains are handsome—solid, immovable, aware of his own weight in the world. He'd agreed to the marriage for trade concessions and fae protection against northern warlords. He didn't know she was fae. He assumed she was a noblewoman from distant provinces, beautiful in the ordinary way. Their wedding night, Lissaen almost told him. The words formed in her throat like honey. She could taste the lie dissolving. But Vethric spoke first: "I know what you are," he said quietly, turning to face her in the candlelight. "My grandmother was fae-touched. I can see the glamour like a veil. I've always seen it."

Lissaen's carefully constructed strategy fractured. "Then why accept the marriage?"

"Because the Twilight Court's choice matters," he said.

stories.title: The Cartographer's Blind Apprentice
stories.text:
Kesla had been mapping the Bleached Wastes for six months before she understood: her master, old Mardoc, was deliberately blinding himself. His eyes remained open, but he refused to look at the maps they charted. Each time a chart was completed, he'd turn away, leaving Kesla to finish the notation. The pattern was deliberate, ritualistic, almost like prayer. "If I see the ninth map," Mardoc finally explained, "the Ascension will trigger. One of us must hold the knowledge without bearing witness. One of us must be the keeper of the unseen."

Kesla was young—only twenty winters—and understood nothing of the cosmic machinery her master described. All she knew was that the Bleached Wastes were singing with frequencies that predated human speech, and that eight previous worlds lay buried in the ash, each one destroyed identically. When Mardoc fell ill in a ruined city beneath the Rust Gate, Kesla made her choice. She completed the ninth map in secret, every line burned into her memory but never committed to parchment.

stories.title: The Singing Merchant's Last Voyage
stories.text:
Kael had collected songs the way other merchants collected spices. She traveled the Sapphire Sea with a crew of tide-touched sailors, trading in melodies that could summon current-shifts and lull sea-serpents to sleep. Her hold was fragrant with salt and harmony. When the deepkin captain Merith emerged from the boundary-waters, Kael recognized her immediately: the woman whose voice Kael had heard singing in her dreams for three years. A song without words, carrying memory and longing and the weight of tides. "You're the one," Merith said, her luminous eyes fixing on Kael with ancient certainty. "My people have been listening. Your melodies reshape the water. You sing the old frequencies."

Kael's crew fell silent. Merging with a deepkin captain meant binding to their realm, leaving the sunlit world forever. Kael should have refused. Instead, she found herself singing—not a song she'd learned, but one that rose from her own depths. A melody of her own making, neither surface nor abyssal, but something between. Merith wept. "That's the song," she breathed. "The song we thought was lost.

stories.title: The Tide-Thief's Shadow
stories.text: In Greyport's shadow-markets, Kess the Thief trafficked in stolen water from before the Remaking—vials that predated the shattering and sold for consciousness-fragments. When the Dominion's glass-tax collectors cornered her in the Saltmere Quarter, she chose to drink the last flask rather than let them confiscate it. The water flooded her veins with pre-Remaking memories: oceans that flowed upward, cities beneath the waves that still sang in Elder frequencies. Now Kess walks between states—half-present, half-drowned—speaking in languages that vanished when the stars reburned. The Deepkin captain sent word: she carries memory they need. The Dominion sent hunters. And Kess, caught between two masters, discovers the water grants her a third option: to fade entirely into the boundary-waters, becoming neither thief nor hunted, but a bridge between worlds.

stories.title: The Wyvern's Gift
stories.text: Kolrith the fragment-hoarder had ruled the eastern peaks for nine centuries, her treasure-vaults singing with gold that now turned to rust and dust. The Remaking stripped her empire to nothing. But in the collapse's aftermath, she discovered something unexpected: a merchant's daughter—locked in her deepest chamber for two hundred years—had never aged. The girl's consciousness was pristine, untouched by time, a relic of the old world. Worth a fortune in fragments. Kolrith could sell her to the deepkin, the Dominion, the bone-singers. Instead, she opened the vault. The girl—who called herself Mera—emerged blinking into a world she didn't recognize, where gold was worthless and dragons hoarded memory instead. Kolrith taught her to navigate the new economies, to taste the bioluminescent inks that traders hawked, to understand that wealth now meant knowing what others had forgotten. When Mera asked why the ancient wyvern had freed her, Kolrith's answer was simple: 'Because I could not eat that kind of hunger anymore.' Mera left the mountains a season later, bound for Verdant Strand.

stories.title: The Singing Geographer
stories.text: Young Henryk the cartographer apprentice had been mapping the Bleached Wastes when the ground began to sing. Not metaphorically—the earth itself hummed with frequencies that matched the bone-singers' ancient melodies. He followed the sound three days' travel east into territory unmarked on any Dominion chart. There, buried in ashen soil, lay a pre-human ruin: pillars of a metal that didn't corrode, inscriptions in Elder Tongue that seemed to shift when he looked away, and at its heart, a crystal that pulsed with the same harmonic that the Drowned Cathedrals had begun emitting weeks before. Henryk realized the Remaking wasn't random catastrophe. It was a pattern—one he'd glimpsed in seven other corrupted maps, all showing identical collapse sequences. He could report to the Dominion, claim credit, become wealthy in fragments. Or he could carry this knowledge to the Counter-Archive, where refugee scribes were quietly documenting the world's true shape. Henryk spent the night in the ruin's shadow, listening to the frequency. By dawn, he'd made his choice.

stories.title: The Oath-Keeper's Daughter
stories.text: Lyris had inherited her father's bondage: a centuries-old oath sworn to the Twilight Court that demanded one descendant per generation serve in the fae realm for seven years. Her turn had come on the spring equinox, 2026-06-04. The summons arrived as frost that would not melt, a cold that spoke in her grandmother's voice. She could refuse—the Fae Courts respected defiance—but then her bloodline would fall under curse for refusing hospitality. Instead, she accepted. The fae lord who received her was Caelith, half-fae exile and prince of a dying court, beautiful and merciless. He tested her with riddles, trials, and impossible choices, each designed to break her will or seduce her loyalty. On the fifth day, he offered her a bargain: stay willingly, become his consort, and her family would be freed from the oath forever. The price was her own freedom. Lyris laughed—not with mockery, but with recognition. She'd seen this trap before, in the tales her grandmother told.

stories.title: The Bone-Singer's Apprentice
stories.text: Moriel was fourteen when Naela the bone-carver found her in the refugee shelter at Verdant Strand, humming an old lullaby in a tongue she'd never learned. The melody was Elder Language—fragments of it, at least—encoded in her blood by a grandmother Moriel had never met. Naela recognized the signs of a latent bone-singer and made an offer: come learn the craft, and you'll be able to guide your family through the Remaking's chaos. What she didn't say: bone-singing costs clarity. Every ritual exacts memory, identity, years of life. Moriel accepted. For months, she carved bone in the hidden workshop beneath Keshtir's Lampwick Quarter, learning to sing the frequencies that moved earth and stone and water. Her voice grew rich; her mind grew thin. She forgot her own name twice before Naela taught her to write it in runes that her bones would carry. On 2026-06-04, Moriel performed her first full working: sealing a fracture in the earth where the Remaking's echo was leaking reality. It took everything—her voice, her memory of Verdant Strand, her childhood entirely.
